title: The First Sunday of Advent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## December 3, 2023 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday of Advent, marking the beginning of the Christian liturgical year in both the Book of Common Prayer and Revised Common Lectionary traditions. The readings would typically include Isaiah 64:1-9 (or similar prophetic texts calling for God's intervention), Psalm 80, 1 Corinthians 1:3-9, and Mark 13:24-37, all emphasizing watchfulness, longing, and Christ's return. Advent represents a season of spiritual preparation and penitential anticipation lasting four Sundays before Christmas, characterized by themes of waiting, hope, and divine judgment.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its 80th General Convention held in Louisville, Kentucky in June-July 2023, where significant decisions on LGBTQ+ inclusion and prayer book revision had been made. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Glenda Shelly (who began her tenure in 2021), was navigating post-convention implementation of these resolutions within parishes. Saint John's Cathedral, as the seat of the diocese, would be central to interpreting and communicating these decisions to the broader Colorado Anglican community during this period.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced its first significant winter weather of the season in late November 2023, with snow and cold temperatures affecting the region in the days leading up to this Sunday. The Colorado legislature was in interim committee work ahead of the 2024 session beginning in January. Locally, the holiday shopping season was in full swing with downtown Denver's holiday decorations and events beginning, including the Christkindl Market that runs through December. No major crises or exceptional events marked the Denver metro area in the specific week prior to December 3.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Biden was managing multiple simultaneous crises: the ongoing war in Gaza (which had intensified dramatically after October 7, 2023) and debates over US military aid, inflation concerns despite recent economic data showing resilience, and early 2024 campaign positioning as his primary challengers emerged. The UAW auto workers' strike that began in September 2023 was concluding with tentative agreements being ratified, marking a significant labor victory. Congress was divided and contentious, with Speaker Mike Johnson newly installed (having taken office in October 2023) and facing government funding deadlines and budget battles heading into the holiday season.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Gaza conflict dominated international news, with the humanitarian crisis deepening following Israel's ground invasion and the scale of civilian casualties becoming increasingly documented and disputed. Ukraine continued its defensive operations against the Russian invasion, now well into its second year, with winter warfare and Western military aid remaining central geopolitical questions. The COP28 climate conference was scheduled to begin in Dubai on November 30, 2023 (just days before this sermon), focusing on fossil fuel phase-out language and climate finance for developing nations.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in early December 2023 was firmly in holiday season mode, with the Thanksgiving-to-Christmas commercial and entertainment push in full motion, though the earlier-than-usual darkness from seasonal change combined with anxieties about Middle Eastern conflict created a more muted national mood than typical festive seasons. The NFL season was in full swing with significant playoff positioning emerging, and the Denver Broncos (struggling through a disappointing 2023 season) were a topic of local sports frustration. Taylor Swift's Eras Tour continued dominating cultural conversation, and streaming services were promoting holiday content; there was a general undercurrent of cultural and political division tempering traditional holiday cheer.
